    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 388
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/388](https://www.erdosproblems.com/388)
    23→
    24→Can one classify all solutions of
    25→$$\prod_{1 \le i \le k_1} (m_1 + i) = \prod_{1 \le j \le k_2} (m_2 + j)$$
    26→where $k_1, k_2 > 3$ and $m_1 + k_1 \le m_2$? Are there only finitely many solutions?
    27→
    28→More generally, if $k_1 > 2$ then for fixed $a$ and $b$,
    29→$$a \cdot \prod_{1 \le i \le k_1} (m_1 + i) = b \cdot \prod_{1 \le j \le k_2} (m_2 + j)$$
    30→should have only a finite number of solutions.
    31→
    32→See also problems 363 and 931.
    33→
    34→[Er76d] Erdős, P.
    35→
    36→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    37→theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    38→
    39→[Er92e] Erdős, P.
    40→-/
    41→
    42→open Finset BigOperators
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos388
    45→
    46→/-- The product of $k$ consecutive natural numbers starting from $m + 1$:
    47→$(m+1)(m+2)\cdots(m+k)$. -/
    48→def consecutiveProduct (m k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    49→  ∏ i ∈ Finset.range k, (m + 1 + i)
    50→
    51→/--
    52→Erdős Problem 388 [Er76d] [ErGr80] [Er92e]:
    53→
    54→Are there only finitely many 4-tuples $(m_1, k_1, m_2, k_2)$ of natural numbers
    55→with $k_1, k_2 > 3$ and $m_1 + k_1 \le m_2$ such that
    56→$$(m_1+1)(m_1+2)\cdots(m_1+k_1) = (m_2+1)(m_2+2)\cdots(m_2+k_2)?$$
    57→-/
    58→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    59→theorem erdos_388 :
    60→    answer(sorry) ↔
    61→    Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
    62→      3 < t.2.1 ∧ 3 < t.2.2.2 ∧
    63→      t.1 + t.2.1 ≤ t.2.2.1 ∧
    64→      consecutiveProduct t.1 t.2.1 = consecutiveProduct t.2.2.1 t.2.2.2} := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→/--
    68→Erdős Problem 388 (General conjecture) [Er76d] [ErGr80] [Er92e]:
    69→
    70→For $k_1 > 2$ and fixed $a, b$, the equation
    71→$$a \cdot \prod_{1 \le i \le k_1} (m_1 + i) = b \cdot \prod_{1 \le j \le k_2} (m_2 + j)$$
    72→should have only finitely many solutions.
    73→-/
    74→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    75→theorem erdos_388.variants.general :
    76→    answer(sorry) ↔
    77→    ∀ (a b : ℕ) (k₁ : ℕ), 2 < k₁ →
    78→    ∀ k₂ : ℕ,
    79→    Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ |
    80→      a * consecutiveProduct t.1 k₁ = b * consecutiveProduct t.2 k₂} := by
    81→  sorry
    82→
    83→end Erdos388
    84→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 363
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/363](https://www.erdosproblems.com/363)
    23→
    24→Is it true that there are only finitely many collections of disjoint intervals
    25→$I_1, \ldots, I_n$ of consecutive integers with $|I_i| \geq 4$ for all $i$, such that
    26→$\prod_i \prod_{m \in I_i} m$ is a perfect square?
    27→
    28→This was disproved:
    29→- [Ul05] Ulas proved infinitely many solutions exist when $n = 4$ or $n \geq 6$, $|I_i| = 4$.
    30→- [BaBe07] Bauer and Bennett proved the same for $n = 3$ and $n = 5$, $|I_i| = 4$.
    31→- [BeVL12] Bennett and Van Luijk found infinitely many solutions for $n \geq 5$, $|I_i| = 5$.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos363
    35→
    36→/-- The product of $4$ consecutive natural numbers starting at $a$. -/
    37→def prod4 (a : ℕ) : ℕ := a * (a + 1) * (a + 2) * (a + 3)
    38→
    39→/--
    40→Erdős Problem 363 (Disproved by Bauer–Bennett [BaBe07]):
    41→
    42→Are there only finitely many triples $(a, b, c)$ of natural numbers with
    43→$a + 4 \leq b$ and $b + 4 \leq c$ (so the three intervals of four consecutive integers
    44→starting at $a$, $b$, $c$ are pairwise disjoint) such that the product of all $12$ elements
    45→is a perfect square? The answer is no.
    46→-/
    47→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    48→theorem erdos_363 : answer(False) ↔
    49→    Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
    50→      t.1 + 4 ≤ t.2.1 ∧ t.2.1 + 4 ≤ t.2.2 ∧
    51→      IsSquare (prod4 t.1 * prod4 t.2.1 * prod4 t.2.2)} := by
    52→  sorry
    53→
    54→end Erdos363
    55→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 931
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/931](https://www.erdosproblems.com/931)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→namespace Erdos931
    26→
    27→/--
    28→Let $k_1 \geq k_2 \geq 3$. Are there only finitely many $n_2\geq n_1 + k_1$
    29→such that
    30→$$
    31→  \prod_{1\leq i\leq k_1}(n_1 + i)\ \text{and}\ \prod_{1\leq j\leq k_2} (n_2 + j)
    32→$$
    33→have the same prime factors?
    34→-/
    35→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    36→theorem erdos_931 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ᵉ (k₁ : ℕ) (k₂ ≥ 3), k₂ ≤ k₁ →
    37→    { (n₁, n₂) | n₁ + k₁ ≤ n₂ ∧
    38→      (∏ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 k₁, (n₁ + i)).primeFactors =
    39→      (∏ j ∈ Finset.Icc 1 k₂, (n₂ + j)).primeFactors }.Finite := by
    40→  sorry
    41→
    42→/--
    43→Erdős thought perhaps if the two products have the same factors then
    44→$n_2 > 2(n_1 + k_1)$.
    45→It is an open question whether this is true when allowing a finite number of counterexamples.
    46→-/
    47→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    48→theorem erdos_931.variants.additional_condition : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ᵉ (k₁ : ℕ) (k₂ ≥ 3), k₂ ≤ k₁ →
    49→    {(n₁, n₂) | n₁ + k₁ ≤ n₂ ∧ n₂ ≤ 2 * (n₁ + k₁) ∧
    50→      (∏ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 k₁, (n₁ + i)).primeFactors =
    51→      (∏ j ∈ Finset.Icc 1 k₂, (n₂ + j)).primeFactors}.Finite := by
    52→  sorry
    53→
    54→/--
    55→In fact there exist counterexamples, like this one found by AlphaProof.
    56→-/
    57→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    58→theorem erdos_931.variants.additional_condition_nonempty : ∃ (k₁ k₂ : ℕ), ∃ (_h₁ : k₂ ≤ k₁), ∃ (_h₂ : 3 ≤ k₂),
    59→  {(n₁, n₂) | n₁ + k₁ ≤ n₂ ∧ n₂ ≤ 2 * (n₁ + k₁) ∧
    60→      (∏ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 k₁, (n₁ + i)).primeFactors =
    61→      (∏ j ∈ Finset.Icc 1 k₂, (n₂ + j)).primeFactors}.Nonempty := by
    62→  use 10, 3, (by norm_num), (by norm_num)
    63→  use (0, 13)
    64→  norm_num [Finset.prod_Icc_succ_top]
    65→  norm_num +decide [Nat.primeFactors, Nat.primeFactorsList]
    66→
    67→/--
    68→Erdős was unable to prove that if the two products have the same factors
    69→then there must exist a prime between $n_1$ and $n_2$.
    70→-/
    71→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    72→theorem erdos_931.variants.exists_prime (k₁ k₂ n₁ n₂ : ℕ) (h₁ : k₂ ≤ k₁) (h₂ : 3 ≤ k₂)
    73→    (h₃ : n₁ + k₁ ≤ n₂) (h₄ : (∏ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 k₁, (n₁ + i)).primeFactors =
    74→      (∏ j ∈ Finset.Icc 1 k₂, (n₂ + j)).primeFactors) :
    75→    ∃ (p : ℕ), p.Prime ∧ n₁ ≤ p ∧ p ≤ n₂ := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos931
    79→

    17→import Lean.Elab
    18→import Lean.Meta.Basic
    19→import FormalConjectures.Util.Answer.Syntax
    20→import Batteries.Lean.Expr
    21→
    22→/-!
    23→# The `answer( )` elaborator
    24→
    25→This file provides syntax for marking up answers in a problem statement.
    26→
    27→Note: certain problems also providing an answer, and can be formalised
    28→using `answer(sorry)` as a placeholder. While providing a proof simply requires
    29→finding any way to replace `:= sorry`, providing an answer is not just finding
    30→any way to replace answer(sorry): it requires evaluation of mathematical meaning,
    31→which is a job for human mathematicians, not Lean alone.
    32→-/
    33→namespace Google
    34→
    35→open Lean Elab Term
    36→
    37→/-- Indicates where the answer is in a problem statement. -/
    38→@[term_elab answer]
    39→def answerElab : TermElab := fun stx expectedType? => do
    40→  match stx with
    41→  | `(answer($a:term)) => do
    42→    mkSaveInfoAnnotation <$> postponeElabTerm (← `(by exact $a)) expectedType?
    43→  | _ => Elab.throwUnsupportedSyntax
    44→
    45→open InfoTree
    46→
    47→/-- An answer: a term, and the context in which it was elaborated -/
    48→structure AnswerInfo where
    49→  ctx : Elab.ContextInfo
    50→  term : Elab.TermInfo
    51→
    52→/-- Print an answer -/
    53→def AnswerInfo.format (a : AnswerInfo) : Elab.Term.TermElabM MessageData :=
    54→  Meta.withMCtx a.ctx.mctx <| Meta.withLCtx a.term.lctx {} do
    55→    let t ← Meta.inferType a.term.expr
    56→    let m ← Meta.mkFreshExprMVar t
    57→    addMessageContextFull m!"{a.term.expr} in context:{indentD m.mvarId!}"
    58→
    59→/-- Find answers by inspecting an `InfoTree` -/
    60→partial def getAnswers {m} [Monad m] (i : InfoTree) : m (Array AnswerInfo) :=
    61→  go none i
    62→where go : _ → InfoTree → _
    63→  | ctx?, context ctx t => go (ctx.mergeIntoOuter? ctx?) t
    64→  | some ctx, node i cs => do
    65→    let ctx := i.updateContext? ctx
    66→    if let .ofTermInfo t := i then
    67→      if t.elaborator == ``answerElab then
    68→        if let .some ctx := ctx then
    69→          return #[⟨ctx, t⟩]
    70→    return (← cs.mapM (go <| i.updateContext? ctx)).toArray.flatten
    71→  | _, _ => pure #[]
    72→
    73→end Google
    74→

content.content:
1→/-
     2→Copyright 2025 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→
     4→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.

    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→open Filter
    26→
    27→open scoped Topology Real
    28→
    29→namespace Erdos1
    30→
    31→/--
    32→A finite set of naturals $A$ is said to be a sum-distinct set for $N \in \mathbb{N}$ if
    33→$A\subseteq\{1, ..., N\}$ and the sums $\sum_{a\in S}a$ are distinct for all $S\subseteq A$
    34→-/
    35→abbrev IsSumDistinctSet (A : Finset ℕ) (N : ℕ) : Prop :=
    36→    A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N ∧ (fun (⟨S, _⟩ : A.powerset) => S.sum id).Injective
    37→
    38→/--
    39→If $A\subseteq\{1, ..., N\}$ with $|A| = n$ is such that the subset sums $\sum_{a\in S}a$ are
    40→distinct for all $S\subseteq A$ then
    41→$$
    42→  N \gg 2 ^ n.
    43→$$
    44→-/
    45→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    46→theorem erdos_1 : ∃ C > (0 : ℝ), ∀ (N : ℕ) (A : Finset ℕ) (_ : IsSumDistinctSet A N),
    47→    N ≠ 0 → C * 2 ^ A.card < N := by
    48→  sorry
    49→
    50→/--
    51→The trivial lower bound is $N \gg 2^n / n$.
    52→-/
    53→@[category undergraduate, AMS 5 11]
    54→theorem erdos_1.variants.weaker : ∃ C > (0 : ℝ), ∀ (N : ℕ) (A : Finset ℕ)
    55→    (_ : IsSumDistinctSet A N), N ≠ 0 → C * 2 ^ A.card / A.card < N := by
    56→  sorry
    57→
    58→/--
    59→Erdős and Moser [Er56] proved
    60→$$
    61→  N \geq (\tfrac{1}{4} - o(1)) \frac{2^n}{\sqrt{n}}.
    62→$$
    63→
    64→[Er56] Erdős, P., _Problems and results in additive number theory_. Colloque sur la Th\'{E}orie des Nombres, Bruxelles, 1955 (1956), 127-137.
    65→-/
    66→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    67→theorem erdos_1.variants.lb : ∃ (o : ℕ → ℝ) (_ : o =o[atTop] (1 : ℕ → ℝ)),
    68→    ∀ (N : ℕ) (A : Finset ℕ) (h : IsSumDistinctSet A N),
    69→      (1 / 4 - o A.card) * 2 ^ A.card / (A.card : ℝ).sqrt ≤ N := by
    70→  sorry
    71→
    72→/--
    73→A number of improvements of the constant $\frac{1}{4}$ have been given, with the current
    74→record $\sqrt{2 / \pi}$ first provied in unpublished work of Elkies and Gleason.
    75→-/
    76→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    77→theorem erdos_1.variants.lb_strong : ∃ (o : ℕ → ℝ) (_ : o =o[atTop] (1 : ℕ → ℝ)),
    78→    ∀ (N : ℕ) (A : Finset ℕ) (h : IsSumDistinctSet A N),
    79→      (√(2 / π) - o A.card) * 2 ^ A.card / (A.card : ℝ).sqrt ≤ N := by
    80→  sorry
    81→
    82→/--
    83→A finite set of real numbers is said to be sum-distinct if all the subset sums differ by
    84→at least $1$.
    85→-/
    86→abbrev IsSumDistinctRealSet (A : Finset ℝ) (N : ℕ) : Prop :=
    87→  ↑A ⊆ Set.Ioc (0 : ℝ) N ∧ (A.powerset : Set (Finset ℝ)).Pairwise fun S₁ S₂ =>
    88→    1 ≤ dist (S₁.sum id) (S₂.sum id)
    89→
    90→/--
    91→A generalisation of the problem to sets $A \subseteq (0, N]$ of real numbers, such that the subset
    92→sums all differ by at least $1$ is proposed in [Er73] and [ErGr80].
    93→
    94→[Er73] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on combinatorial number theory_. A survey of combinatorial theory (Proc. Internat. Sympos., Colorado State Univ., Fort Collins, Colo., 1971) (1973), 117-138.
    95→
    96→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory_. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    97→-/
    98→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    99→theorem erdos_1.variants.real : ∃ C > (0 : ℝ), ∀ (N : ℕ) (A : Finset ℝ)
   100→    (_ : IsSumDistinctRealSet A N), N ≠ 0 → C * 2 ^ A.card < N := by
   101→  sorry
   102→
   103→/--
   104→The minimal value of $N$ such that there exists a sum-distinct set with three
   105→elements is $4$.
   106→
   107→https://oeis.org/A276661
   108→-/
   109→@[category undergraduate, AMS 5 11]
   110→theorem erdos_1.variants.least_N_3 :
   111→    IsLeast { N | ∃ A, IsSumDistinctSet A N ∧ A.card = 3 } 4 := by
   112→  refine ⟨⟨{1, 2, 4}, ?_⟩, ?_⟩
   113→  · simp
   114→    refine ⟨by decide, ?_⟩
   115→    let P := Finset.powerset {1, 2, 4}
   116→    have : Finset.univ.image (fun p : P ↦ ∑ x ∈ p, x) = {0, 1, 2, 4, 3, 5, 6, 7} := by
   117→      refine Finset.ext_iff.mpr (fun n => ?_)
   118→      simp [show P = {{}, {1}, {2}, {4}, {1, 2}, {1, 4}, {2, 4}, {1, 2, 4}} by decide]
   119→      omega
   120→    rw [← Set.injOn_univ, ← Finset.coe_univ]
   121→    have : (Finset.univ.image (fun p : P ↦ ∑ x ∈ p.1, x)).card = (Finset.univ (α := P)).card := by
   122→      rw [this]; aesop
   123→    exact Finset.injOn_of_card_image_eq this
   124→  · simp [mem_lowerBounds]
   125→    intro n S h h_inj hcard3
   126→    by_contra hn
   127→    interval_cases n; aesop; aesop
   128→    · have := Finset.card_le_card h
   129→      aesop
   130→    · absurd h_inj
   131→      rw [(Finset.subset_iff_eq_of_card_le (Nat.le_of_eq (by rw [hcard3]; decide))).mp h]
   132→      decide
   133→
   134→/--
   135→The minimal value of $N$ such that there exists a sum-distinct set with five
   136→elements is $13$.
   137→
   138→https://oeis.org/A276661
   139→-/
   140→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
   141→theorem erdos_1.variants.least_N_5 :
   142→    IsLeast { N | ∃ A, IsSumDistinctSet A N ∧ A.card = 5 } 13 := by
   143→  sorry
   144→
   145→/--
   146→The minimal value of $N$ such that there exists a sum-distinct set with nine
   147→elements is $161$.
   148→
   149→https://oeis.org/A276661
   150→-/
   151→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
   152→theorem erdos_1.variants.least_N_9 :
   153→    IsLeast { N | ∃ A, IsSumDistinctSet A N ∧ A.card = 9 } 161 := by
   154→  sorry
   155→
   156→end Erdos1
   157→

    14→limitations under the License.

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1005
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1005](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1005)
    23→
    24→Let $a_1/b_1, a_2/b_2, \ldots$ be the Farey fractions of order $n \geq 4$. Let $f(n)$ be
    25→the largest integer such that for all pairs of indices $k < l$ with $l \leq k + f(n)$,
    26→the fractions $a_k/b_k$ and $a_l/b_l$ are "similarly ordered":
    27→$(a_k - a_l)(b_k - b_l) \geq 0$.
    28→
    29→[Er43] Erdős, P., *On the distribution of the convergents of almost all real numbers*.
    30→Bull. Amer. Math. Soc. (1943).
    31→
    32→[vD25b] van Doorn, T. (2025).
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open Filter Finset
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos1005
    38→
    39→/-- The Farey fractions of order $n$: all reduced fractions $a/b \in [0,1]$ with
    40→$b \leq n$, as a sorted list of rationals. -/
    41→def fareyFractions (n : ℕ) : List ℚ :=
    42→  ((Finset.Icc 1 n).biUnion fun b =>
    43→    ((Finset.range (b + 1)).filter fun a => Nat.Coprime a b).image
    44→      fun (a : ℕ) => (a : ℚ) / (b : ℚ)).sort (· ≤ ·)
    45→
    46→/-- Two Farey fractions $p = a_1/b_1$ and $q = a_2/b_2$ (in lowest terms) are
    47→similarly ordered if $(a_1 - a_2)(b_1 - b_2) \geq 0$. -/
    48→def similarlyOrdered (p q : ℚ) : Prop :=
    49→  (p.num - q.num) * ((p.den : ℤ) - q.den) ≥ 0
    50→
    51→/-- $f(n)$: the largest $d$ such that for all pairs of indices $i < j$ with $j \leq i + d$
    52→in the Farey sequence of order $n$, the fractions are similarly ordered. -/
    53→noncomputable def fareySimOrderFn (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    54→  sSup {d : ℕ | ∀ i j : ℕ, i < j → j ≤ i + d →
    55→    ∀ (hi : i < (fareyFractions n).length) (hj : j < (fareyFractions n).length),
    56→    similarlyOrdered ((fareyFractions n).get ⟨i, hi⟩) ((fareyFractions n).get ⟨j, hj⟩)}
    57→
    58→/--
    59→Erdős Problem 1005 [Er43]:
    60→
    61→There exists a constant $c > 0$ such that $f(n)/n \to c$ as $n \to \infty$, where $f(n)$ is
    62→the largest window size for similarly ordered consecutive Farey fractions of
    63→order $n$.
    64→
    65→van Doorn [vD25b] proved $(1/12 - o(1))n \leq f(n) \leq n/4 + O(1)$ and conjectures
    66→$c = 1/4$.
    67→-/
    68→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    69→theorem erdos_1005 :
    70→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    71→    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (fareySimOrderFn n : ℝ) / (n : ℝ)) atTop (nhds c) := by
    72→  sorry
    73→
    74→end Erdos1005

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 10
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/10](https://www.erdosproblems.com/10)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→namespace Erdos10
    26→
    27→/--
    28→The set of natural numbers that can be written as a sum
    29→of a prime and at most $k$ powers of $2$.
    30→-/
    31→abbrev sumPrimeAndTwoPows (k : ℕ) : Set ℕ :=
    32→  { p + (pows.map (2 ^ ·)).sum | (p : ℕ) (pows : Multiset ℕ) (_ : p.Prime)
    33→    (_ : pows.card ≤ k)}
    34→
    35→/--
    36→Is there some $k$ such that every integer is the sum of a prime and at most $k$
    37→powers of $2$?
    38→-/
    39→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    40→theorem erdos_10 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ k, sumPrimeAndTwoPows k = Set.univ \ {0, 1} := by
    41→  sorry
    42→
    43→/--
    44→Gallagher [Ga75] has shown that for any $ϵ > 0$ there exists $k(ϵ)$
    45→such that the set of integers which are the sum of a prime and at most $k(ϵ)$
    46→many powers of $2$ has lower density at least $1 - ϵ$.
    47→
    48→Ref: Gallagher, P. X., _Primes and powers of 2_.
    49→-/
    50→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    51→theorem erdos_10.variants.gallagher (ε : ℝ)
    52→    (hε : 0 < ε) : ∃ k, 1 - ε ≤ (sumPrimeAndTwoPows k).lowerDensity := by
    53→  sorry
    54→
    55→/--
    56→Granville and Soundararajan [GrSo98] have conjectured that at most $3$
    57→powers of $2$ suffice for all odd integers, and hence at most $4$ powers of $2$
    58→suffice for all even integers.
    59→
    60→Ref: Granville, A. and Soundararajan, K., _A Binary Additive Problem of Erdős and the Order of $2$ mod $p^2$_
    61→-/
    62→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    63→theorem erdos_10.variants.granville_soundararajan_odd :
    64→    {n : ℕ | Odd n ∧ 1 < n} ⊆ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 3 ∧
    65→      {n : ℕ | Even n ∧ n ≠ 0} ⊆ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 4 := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→/--
    69→Bogdan Grechuk has observed that `1117175146` is not the sum of a prime
    70→and at most $3$ powers of $2$.
    71→-/
    72→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    73→theorem erdos_10.variants.grechuk_example :
    74→    1117175146 ∉ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 3 := by
    75→  sorry
    76→
    77→/--
    78→There are infinitely many even integers not the sum of a prime and $2$ powers of $2$
    79→-/
    80→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    81→theorem erdos_10.variants.two_pows :
    82→    Set.Infinite <| {n : ℕ | Even n} \ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 2 := by
    83→  sorry
    84→
    85→/--
    86→Bogdan Grechuk has observed that $1117175146$ is not the sum of a prime and at most $3$
    87→powers of $2$, and pointed out that parity considerations, coupled with the fact that there
    88→are many integers not the sum of a prime and $2$ powers of $2$ suggest that there exist
    89→infinitely many even integers which are not the sum of a prime and at most $3$ powers of $2$).
    90→-/
    91→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    92→theorem erdos_10.variants.grechuk :
    93→    Set.Infinite <| {n : ℕ | Even n} \ sumPrimeAndTwoPows 3 := by
    94→  sorry
    95→
    96→end Erdos10

    27→A set $A$ is said to be sum-free if the sumset $A + A$ is disjoint from $A$, i.e.
    28→if the equation $a + b = c$ has no solution with $a, b, c \in A$.
    29→-/
    30→def IsSumFree (A : Set α) : Prop := Disjoint (A + A) A
    31→
    32→/-- A set $A$ of natural numbers is said to have bounded gaps if there exists an integer $p$ such
    33→that $A ∩ [n, n + 1, ..., n + p]$ is nonempty for all $n$. -/
    34→def IsSyndetic (A : Set ℕ) : Prop := ∃ p, ∀ n, (A ∩ .Icc n (n + p)).Nonempty
    35→
    36→/-- A Sidon set is a set, such that such that all pairwise sums of elements are distinct apart from
    37→coincidences forced by the commutativity of addition. -/
    38→def IsSidon (A : Set α) : Prop := ∀ᵉ (i₁ ∈ A) (j₁ ∈ A) (i₂ ∈ A) (j₂ ∈ A),

    53→  have ha : a ∈ A := mem_of_mem_inter_left <| hss (hY ▸ ⟨0, by norm_num, by simp⟩)
    54→  have ha₁ : a + d ∈ A := mem_of_mem_inter_left <| hss (hY ▸ ⟨1, by norm_num, by simp⟩)
    55→  have ha₂ : a + 2 • d ∈ A := mem_of_mem_inter_left <| hss (hY ▸ ⟨2, by norm_num, by simp⟩)
    56→  have := hA _ ha _ ha₁ _ ha₂ _ ha₁ (by simp; omega)

    62→  fun _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ ↦ hB _ (hAB ‹_›) _ (hAB ‹_›) _ (hAB ‹_›) _ (hAB ‹_›) ‹_›
    63→
    64→theorem IsSidon.insert {A : Set α} {m : α} [IsRightCancelAdd α] [IsLeftCancelAdd α]
    65→    (hA : IsSidon A) :
    66→    IsSidon (A ∪ {m}) ↔ (m ∈ A ∨ ∀ᵉ (a ∈ A) (b ∈ A), m + m ≠ a + b ∧ ∀ c ∈ A, m + a ≠ b + c) := by
    67→  by_cases h_mem : m ∈ A
    68→  · exact ⟨fun _ ↦ .inl h_mem, fun _ ↦ by rwa [union_singleton, insert_eq_of_mem h_mem]⟩
    69→  refine ⟨fun h ↦ .inr fun a ha b hb ↦ ⟨fun hc ↦ ?_, fun c hc h_contr ↦ ?_⟩, fun hm ↦ ?_⟩
    70→  · exact h m (by simp) a (by simp [ha]) m (by simp) b (by simp [hb]) hc
    71→      |>.elim (fun _ ↦ by simp_all) (fun _ ↦ by simp_all)
    72→  · exact h m (by simp) b (by simp [hb]) a (by simp [ha]) c (by simp [hc]) h_contr
    73→      |>.elim (fun _ ↦ by simp_all) (fun _ ↦ by simp_all)
    74→  · intro i₁ hi₁
    75→    rcases hi₁ with (hi₁ | hi₁)
    76→    · intro j₁ hj₁
    77→      rcases hj₁ with (hj₁ | hj₁)
    78→      · intro i₂ hi₂
    79→        rcases hi₂ with (hi₂ | hi₂)
    80→        · intro j₂ hj₂
    81→          rcases hj₂ with (hj₂ | hj₂)
    82→          · exact fun h ↦ hA i₁ hi₁ j₁ hj₁ i₂ hi₂ j₂ hj₂ h
    83→          · simp_all
    84→            exact fun h ↦ by cases (hm j₁ hj₁ i₁ hi₁).2 i₂ hi₂ (add_comm j₁ m ▸ h.symm)
    85→        · simp_all
    86→          exact fun a ha h ↦ by cases (hm i₁ hi₁ j₁ hj₁).2 a ha (add_comm i₁ m ▸ h)
    87→      · simp_all
    88→        refine ⟨fun b hb h ↦ .inr <| by simp_all [add_comm], fun b hb ↦ ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, ?_⟩⟩
    89→        · cases (hm i₁ hi₁ b hb).1 h.symm
    90→        · exact fun c hc h ↦ by cases ((hm c hc i₁ hi₁).2 b hb) h.symm
    91→    · simp_all
    92→      exact fun _ _ _ _ _ ↦ by simp_all [add_comm]
    93→
    94→
    95→/-!
    96→Maximal Sidon sets in an interval.
    97→
    98→We follow the convention that `IsMaximalSidonSetIn A N` means `A ⊆ {1, …, N}` is Sidon and
    99→is inclusion-maximal among subsets of `Set.Icc 1 N` with the Sidon property.
   100→-/
   101→
   102→/-- `IsMaximalSidonSetIn A N` means `A ⊆ {1, …, N}` is Sidon and cannot be extended within
   103→`{1, …, N}` while remaining Sidon. -/
   104→def IsMaximalSidonSetIn (A : Set ℕ) (N : ℕ) : Prop :=
   105→  A ⊆ Set.Icc 1 N ∧ IsSidon A ∧
   106→    ∀ ⦃x : ℕ⦄, x ∈ Set.Icc 1 N → x ∉ A → ¬ IsSidon (A ∪ {x})
   107→
   108→namespace IsMaximalSidonSetIn
   109→
   110→/-- If `A` is a maximal Sidon set in `{1, …, N}`, then `A ⊆ {1, …, N}`. -/
   111→theorem subset {A : Set ℕ} {N : ℕ} (hA : IsMaximalSidonSetIn A N) :
   112→    A ⊆ Set.Icc 1 N := hA.1
   113→
   114→/-- If `A` is a maximal Sidon set in `{1, …, N}`, then `A` is Sidon. -/
   115→theorem isSidon {A : Set ℕ} {N : ℕ} (hA : IsMaximalSidonSetIn A N) : IsSidon A := hA.2.1
   116→
   117→/-- Maximality condition unpacked. -/
   118→theorem maximal {A : Set ℕ} {N : ℕ} (hA : IsMaximalSidonSetIn A N) {x : ℕ}
   119→    (hx : x ∈ Set.Icc 1 N) (hxA : x ∉ A) : ¬ IsSidon (A ∪ {x}) := hA.2.2 hx hxA
   120→
   121→end IsMaximalSidonSetIn
   122→
   123→end Set
   124→
   125→namespace Finset
   126→
   127→instance (A : Finset α) [DecidableEq α] : Decidable (IsSidon (A : Set α)) :=
   128→  decidable_of_iff (∀ᵉ (i₁ ∈ A) (j₁ ∈ A) (i₂ ∈ A) (j₂ ∈ A), _) <| by rfl
   129→
   130→/-- The maximum size of a Sidon set in the supplied `Finset`. -/
   131→def maxSidonSubsetCard (A : Finset α) [DecidableEq α] : ℕ :=
   132→  (A.powerset.filter fun B : Finset α ↦ IsSidon (B : Set α)).sup Finset.card
   133→
   134→/-- If `A` is finite Sidon, then `A ∪ {s}` is also Sidon provided `s ≥ A.max + 1`. -/
   135→theorem IsSidon.insert_ge_max' {A : Finset ℕ} (h : A.Nonempty) (hA : IsSidon (A : Set ℕ)) {s : ℕ}
   136→    (hs : 2 * A.max' h + 1 ≤ s) :
   137→    IsSidon (A ∪ {s}) := by
   138→  have h₁ {a b c : ℕ} (ha : a ∈ A) (hb : b ∈ A) (hc : c ∈ A) :
   139→        a + b < 2 * A.max' h + 1 + c := by linarith [A.le_max' _ ha, A.le_max' _ hb]
   140→  have : s ∉ A := by
   141→    exact mt (A.le_max' _) <| not_le.2 <| Finset.max'_lt_iff _ ‹_› |>.2 fun a ha ↦ by
   142→      linarith [A.le_max' _ ha]
   143→  exact (IsSidon.insert hA).2 <| by simpa [this] using fun a ha b hb ↦
   144→    ⟨by linarith [A.le_max' _ ha, A.le_max' _ hb], fun c hc ↦ by linarith [h₁ hc hb ha]⟩
   145→
   146→theorem IsSidon.exists_insert {A : Finset ℕ} (h : A.Nonempty) (hA : IsSidon (A : Set ℕ)) :
   147→    ∃ m ∉ A, IsSidon (A ∪ {m}) := by
   148→  refine ⟨2 * A.max' h + 1, ?_, insert_ge_max' h hA le_rfl⟩
   149→  exact mt (A.le_max' _) <| not_le.2 <| Finset.max'_lt_iff _ ‹_› |>.2 fun a ha ↦ by
   150→    linarith [A.le_max' _ ha]
   151→
   152→theorem IsSidon.exists_insert_ge {A : Finset ℕ} (h : A.Nonempty) (hA : IsSidon (A : Set ℕ)) (s : ℕ) :
   153→    ∃ m ≥ s, m ∉ A ∧ IsSidon (A ∪ {m}) := by
   154→  refine ⟨if s ≥ 2 * A.max' h + 1 then s else 2 * A.max' h + 1, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
   155→  · split_ifs <;> linarith
   156→  · split_ifs <;>
   157→    exact mt (A.le_max' _) <| not_le.2 <| Finset.max'_lt_iff _ ‹_› |>.2 fun a ha ↦ by
   158→      linarith [A.le_max' _ ha]
   159→  · split_ifs with hs
   160→    · exact insert_ge_max' h hA hs
   161→    · exact insert_ge_max' h hA le_rfl
   162→
   163→end Finset
   164→

    16→
    17→import Mathlib.Algebra.Module.NatInt
    18→import Mathlib.Data.ENat.Lattice
    19→import Mathlib.Data.Set.Card
    20→import Mathlib.Tactic.IntervalCases
    21→
    22→open scoped Classical
    23→
    24→/-! # Arithmetic Progressions
    25→
    26→Main definitions:
    27→- `Set.IsAPOfLengthWith (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) (a d : α)` : predicate asserting that `s` is the
    28→  set consisting of an arithmetic progression of length `l` (possibly infinite) with first term
    29→  `a` and difference `d`. Useful for cases in which additional conditions need to be applied to
    30→  the individual terms and/or difference.
    31→- `Set.IsAPOfLength (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞)` : predicate asserting that `s` is the set consisting
    32→  of an arithmetic progression of length `l`, for some some first term and difference.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→variable {α : Type*} [AddCommMonoid α]
    36→
    37→/--
    38→A set $S$ is an arithmetic progression of length $l$ with first term $a$ and difference $d$
    39→if $S = \{a, a + d, ..., a + (l - 1)d\}$, if $l$ if finite, else $S = \{a, a + d, a + 2d, ...\}.
    40→This can be written as `Set.IsAPOfLengthWith a l a d`, where `l : ℕ∞` may take the infinite
    41→value `⊤`.
    42→-/
    43→def Set.IsAPOfLengthWith (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) (a d : α) : Prop :=
    44→  ENat.card s = l ∧ s = {a + n • d | (n : ℕ) (_ : n < l)}
    45→
    46→/--
    47→A list version of `Set.IsAPOfLengthWith`. Useful when order preservation is required, for example
    48→when considering images under arbitrary functions.
    49→-/
    50→def List.IsAPOfLengthWith (s : List α) (l : ℕ) (a d : α) : Prop :=
    51→  s = (List.range l).map (fun n ↦ a + n • d) ∨ s = (List.range l).reverse.map (fun n ↦ a + n • d)
    52→
    53→namespace Set.IsAPOfLengthWith
    54→
    55→variable {s : Set α} {l : ℕ∞} {a d : α}
    56→
    57→theorem card (h : s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d) : ENat.card s = l := h.1
    58→theorem eq (h : s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d) : s = {a + n • d | (n : ℕ) (_ : n < l)} := h.2
    59→
    60→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length zero if and only
    61→if `s` is empty. -/
    62→@[simp]
    63→theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 0 a d ↔ s = ∅ := by simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    64→
    65→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length one if and only
    66→if `s` is a singleton. -/
    67→@[simp]
    68→theorem one : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 1 a d ↔ s = {a} := by simp +contextual [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    69→
    70→end Set.IsAPOfLengthWith
    71→
    72→namespace List.IsAPOfLengthWith
    73→
    74→variable {s : List α} {l : ℕ} {a d : α}
    75→
    76→theorem length (h : s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d) : s.length = l := by
    77→  cases h <;> simp_all
    78→
    79→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length zero if and only
    80→if `s` is empty. -/
    81→@[simp]
    82→theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 0 a d ↔ s = [] := by
    83→  simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    84→
    85→/-- An arithmetic progression with first term `a` and difference `d` is of length one if and only
    86→if `s` is a singleton. -/
    87→@[simp]
    88→theorem one : s.IsAPOfLengthWith 1 a d ↔ s = [a] := by
    89→  simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    90→
    91→end List.IsAPOfLengthWith
    92→
    93→/-- In an abelian additive group `α`, the set `{a, b}` with `a ≠ b` is an arithmetic progression of
    94→length `2` with first term `a` and difference `b - a`. -/
    95→theorem Set.isAPOfLengthWith_pair {α : Type*} [DecidableEq α] [AddCommGroup α]
    96→    {a b : α} (hab : a ≠ b) :
    97→    Set.IsAPOfLengthWith {a, b} 2 a (b - a) := by
    98→  simp [IsAPOfLengthWith]
    99→  rw [Finset.card_insert_of_notMem (by simpa only [Finset.mem_singleton])]
   100→  simp
   101→  refine Set.ext fun x => ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, fun ⟨n, ⟨_, _⟩⟩ ↦ by interval_cases n <;> simp_all⟩
   102→  cases h with
   103→  | inl hl => use 0; simp [hl]
   104→  | inr hr => exact ⟨1, by norm_num, by simp_all⟩
   105→
   106→-- Formalisation note: separate result needed for `ℕ` since this is not covered by
   107→-- the `AddCommGroup` result above.
   108→/-- The set `{a, b} : Set ℕ` with `a < b` is an arithmetic progression of length `2` with
   109→first term `a` and difference `b - a`. -/
   110→theorem Nat.isAPOfLengthWith_pair {a b : ℕ} (hab : a < b) :
   111→    Set.IsAPOfLengthWith {a, b} 2 a (b - a) := by
   112→  let ⟨n, h⟩ := Nat.exists_eq_add_of_lt hab
   113→  simp [Set.IsAPOfLengthWith, h, add_assoc]
   114→  exact Set.ext fun x => ⟨fun a => by aesop, fun ⟨w, ⟨_, _⟩⟩ => by interval_cases w <;> simp_all⟩
   115→
   116→/--
   117→The predicate that a set `s` is an arithmetic progression of length `l` (possibly infinite).
   118→This predicate does not assert a specific value for the first term or the difference of the
   119→arithmetic progression.
   120→-/
   121→def Set.IsAPOfLength (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) : Prop :=
   122→  ∃ a d : α, s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d
   123→
   124→/--
   125→A list version of `Set.IsAPOfLength`. Useful when order preservation is required, for example
   126→when considering images under arbitrary functions.
   127→-/
   128→def List.IsAPOfLength (s : List α) (l : ℕ) : Prop :=
   129→  ∃ a d : α, s.IsAPOfLengthWith l a d
   130→
   131→namespace Set.IsAPOfLength
   132→
   133→open Set.IsAPOfLengthWith
   134→
   135→variable {s : Set α} {l : ℕ∞}
   136→
   137→theorem card (h : s.IsAPOfLength l) : ENat.card s = l := h.choose_spec.choose_spec.1
   138→
   139→theorem eq (h : s.IsAPOfLength l) : ∃ a d : α, s = {a + n • d | (n : ℕ) (_ : n < l)} :=
   140→  ⟨h.choose, h.choose_spec.choose, h.choose_spec.choose_spec.2⟩
   141→
   142→/-- Only the empty set is a finite arithmetic progression of length $0$. -/
   143→@[simp] theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLength 0 ↔ s = ∅ := by simp [Set.IsAPOfLength]
   144→
   145→/-- Only singletons are finite arithmetic progressions of length $1$. -/
   146→@[simp] theorem one : s.IsAPOfLength 1 ↔ ∃ a, s = {a} := by simp [IsAPOfLength]
   147→
   148→/-- If a set is an arithmetic progression of lengths `l₁` and `l₂`, then the lengths are
   149→equal. -/
   150→theorem congr {s : Set α} {l₁ l₂ : ℕ∞}
   151→    (h₁ : s.IsAPOfLength l₁) (h₂ : s.IsAPOfLength l₂) :
   152→    l₁ = l₂ := by
   153→  rw [← h₁.card, h₂.card]
   154→
   155→end Set.IsAPOfLength
   156→
   157→namespace List.IsAPOfLength
   158→
   159→open List.IsAPOfLengthWith
   160→
   161→variable {s : List α} {l : ℕ}
   162→
   163→theorem length (h : s.IsAPOfLength l) : s.length = l := by
   164→  obtain ⟨_, _, h⟩ := h
   165→  exact h.length
   166→
   167→/-- Only the empty list is a finite arithmetic progression of length $0$. -/
   168→@[simp] theorem zero : s.IsAPOfLength 0 ↔ s = [] := by simp [IsAPOfLength]
   169→
   170→/-- Only singletons are finite arithmetic progressions of length $1$. -/
   171→@[simp] theorem one : s.IsAPOfLength 1 ↔ ∃ a, s = [a] := by simp [IsAPOfLength]
   172→
   173→/-- If a list is an arithmetic progression of lengths `l₁` and `l₂`, then the lengths are
   174→equal. -/
   175→theorem congr {s : List α} {l₁ l₂ : ℕ}
   176→    (h₁ : s.IsAPOfLength l₁) (h₂ : s.IsAPOfLength l₂) :
   177→    l₁ = l₂ := by
   178→  rw [← h₁.length, h₂.length]
   179→
   180→end List.IsAPOfLength
   181→
   182→theorem Set.isAPOfLength_pair {α : Type*} [DecidableEq α] [AddCommGroup α] {a b : α} (hab : a ≠ b) :
   183→    Set.IsAPOfLength {a, b} 2 :=
   184→  ⟨a, b - a, Set.isAPOfLengthWith_pair hab⟩
   185→
   186→theorem Nat.isAPOfLength_pair {a b : ℕ} (hab : a < b) :
   187→    Set.IsAPOfLength {a, b} 2 :=
   188→  ⟨a, b - a, Nat.isAPOfLengthWith_pair hab⟩
   189→
   190→/-- The empty set is not an arithmetic progression of positive length. -/
   191→theorem Set.not_isAPOfLength_empty {l : ℕ∞} (hl : 0 < l) :
   192→    ¬Set.IsAPOfLength (∅ : Set α) l :=
   193→  fun h ↦ by simp_all [h.congr <| Set.IsAPOfLength.zero.2 rfl]
   194→
   195→/-- We say that a set `s` is free of arithmetic progressions of length `l` if `s` contains no
   196→non-trivial arithmetic progressions of length `l`. Written as `Set.IsAPOfLengthFree s l`. --/
   197→def Set.IsAPOfLengthFree (s : Set α) (l : ℕ∞) : Prop :=
   198→  ∀ t ⊆ s, t.IsAPOfLength l → l ≤ 1
   199→
   200→/-- Any set is free of arithmetic progressions of length `1`, because such APs are all trivial. -/
   201→theorem Set.isAPOfLengthFree_one (s : Set α) : s.IsAPOfLengthFree 1 := by
   202→  simp [Set.IsAPOfLengthFree]
   203→
   204→/-- Any set is free of arithmetic progressions of length `0`, because such APs are all trivial. -/
   205→theorem Set.isAPOfLengthFree_zero (s : Set α) : s.IsAPOfLengthFree 0 := by
   206→  simp [Set.IsAPOfLengthFree]
   207→
   208→/-- Any non-trivial arithmetic progression cannot be free of arithmetic progressions. -/
   209→theorem Set.IsAPOfLength.not_isAPOfLengthFree {s : Set α} {l : ℕ∞}
   210→    (hs : s.IsAPOfLength l) (hl : 1 < l) : ¬s.IsAPOfLengthFree l := by
   211→  simpa [Set.IsAPOfLengthFree] using ⟨s, le_rfl, ⟨hs, hl⟩⟩
   212→
   213→/--
   214→Define the largest possible size of a subset of $\{1, \dots, N\}$ that does not contain
   215→any non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progression.
   216→-/
   217→noncomputable def Set.IsAPOfLengthFree.maxCard (k : ℕ) (N : ℕ) : ℕ :=
   218→  sSup {Finset.card S | (S) (_ : S ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N) (_ : (S : Set ℕ).IsAPOfLengthFree k)}
   219→
   220→theorem Set.IsAPOfLengthFree.maxCard_zero (N : ℕ) : maxCard 0 N = N := by
   221→  simp only [maxCard, Nat.cast_zero, isAPOfLengthFree_zero, exists_const, exists_prop]
   222→  apply IsGreatest.csSup_eq
   223→  refine ⟨⟨Finset.Icc 1 N, Subset.rfl, Nat.card_Icc _ _⟩, ?_⟩
   224→  rintro n ⟨S, hS, rfl⟩
   225→  exact S.card_mono hS |>.trans_eq (Nat.card_Icc _ _)
   226→
   227→theorem Set.IsAPOfLengthFree.maxCard_one (N : ℕ) : maxCard 1 N = N := by
   228→  nth_rw 2 [← maxCard_zero N]
   229→  simp [maxCard, isAPOfLengthFree_one, isAPOfLengthFree_zero]
   230→
   231→/-- A set `A` contains an arithmetic progression of length `k` with difference `d`. -/
   232→def Set.ContainsAP (A : Set α) (k : ℕ) (d : α) : Prop :=
   233→  ∃ a, ∃ s, s ⊆ A ∧ s.IsAPOfLengthWith (k : ℕ∞) a d
   234→
   235→def ContainsMonoAPofLength {κ : Type} [Finite κ] {M : Set α}
   236→    (coloring : M → κ) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
   237→  ∃ c : κ, ∃ ap : Set M, ((·.1) '' ap).IsAPOfLength k ∧
   238→    ∀ m ∈ ap, coloring m = c
   239→
   240→/--
   241→A function `f : α → β` has a monotone `k`-term arithmetic progression if there exists an
   242→arithmetic progression `l` of length `k` in `α` such that its image under `f` is sorted.
   243→-/
   244→def HasMonotoneAP {β : Type*} [Preorder β] (f : α → β) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
   245→  ∃ l : List α, l.IsAPOfLength k ∧ (l.map f).Pairwise (· < ·)
   246→
   247→/--
   248→Define the largest possible size of a subset of a finset `s` that does not contain
   249→any non-trivial `k`-term arithmetic progression.
   250→-/
   251→noncomputable def Finset.maxAPFreeCard (k : ℕ) (s : Finset α) : ℕ :=
   252→  (s.powerset.filter fun t : Finset α ↦ (t : Set α).IsAPOfLengthFree k).sup Finset.card
   253→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Primitive elements of a set with respect to divisibility
    21→
    22→An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ⊆ ℕ` if `n ∈ S` and no proper divisor of `n` is in `S`.
    23→
    24→This concept appears naturally when studying sequences closed under "divisor inheritance" -
    25→if every member `n` of a sequence has the property that `m * n` is also in the sequence
    26→for coprime `m`, then the primitive elements generate the entire sequence.
    27→
    28→## Main definitions
    29→
    30→* `Set.IsPrimitive`: A natural number `n` is primitive in `S` if `n ∈ S` and
    31→  `n.properDivisors` is disjoint from `S`.
    32→* `Set.primitives`: The set of primitive elements of `S`.
    33→
    34→## Main results
    35→
    36→* `Set.IsPrimitive.mem`: A primitive element is in the set.
    37→* `Set.IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor`: Proper divisors of a primitive element are not
    38→  in the set.
    39→* `Set.one_isPrimitive_iff`: `1` is primitive in `S` iff `1 ∈ S`.
    40→* `Set.prime_isPrimitive_iff`: A prime `p` is primitive in `S` iff `p ∈ S` and `1 ∉ S`.
    41→-/
    42→
    43→namespace Set
    44→
    45→/-- An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ⊆ ℕ` if `n ∈ S` and no proper divisor of `n`
    46→is in `S`. -/
    47→def IsPrimitive (S : Set ℕ) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    48→  n ∈ S ∧ Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set ℕ) S
    49→
    50→/-- The set of primitive elements of `S`. -/
    51→def primitives (S : Set ℕ) : Set ℕ :=
    52→  {n | S.IsPrimitive n}
    53→
    54→theorem IsPrimitive.mem {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) : n ∈ S := h.1
    55→
    56→theorem IsPrimitive.disjoint_properDivisors {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) :
    57→    Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set ℕ) S := h.2
    58→
    59→theorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor {S : Set ℕ} {n d : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    60→    (hd : d ∈ n.properDivisors) : d ∉ S :=
    61→  Set.disjoint_left.mp h.2 hd
    62→
    63→theorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_dvd_of_lt {S : Set ℕ} {n d : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    64→    (hdvd : d ∣ n) (hlt : d < n) : d ∉ S :=
    65→  h.not_mem_of_properDivisor (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mpr ⟨hdvd, hlt⟩)
    66→
    67→theorem isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} :
    68→    S.IsPrimitive n ↔ n ∈ S ∧ ∀ d ∈ n.properDivisors, d ∉ S := by
    69→  simp only [IsPrimitive, Set.disjoint_left, Finset.mem_coe]
    70→
    71→theorem isPrimitive_iff' {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} (_hn : 0 < n) :
    72→    S.IsPrimitive n ↔ n ∈ S ∧ ∀ d, d ∣ n → d < n → d ∉ S := by
    73→  rw [isPrimitive_iff]
    74→  constructor <;> intro ⟨hmem, hdiv⟩
    75→  · exact ⟨hmem, fun d hd hlt => hdiv d (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mpr ⟨hd, hlt⟩)⟩
    76→  · exact ⟨hmem, fun d hd => hdiv d (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd).1 (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd).2⟩
    77→
    78→theorem mem_primitives_iff {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} : n ∈ S.primitives ↔ S.IsPrimitive n := Iff.rfl
    79→
    80→@[simp]
    81→theorem primitives_subset {S : Set ℕ} : S.primitives ⊆ S := fun _ h => h.mem
    82→
    83→theorem one_isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ℕ} : S.IsPrimitive 1 ↔ 1 ∈ S := by
    84→  simp [isPrimitive_iff, Nat.properDivisors_one]
    85→
    86→theorem prime_isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ℕ} {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime) :
    87→    S.IsPrimitive p ↔ p ∈ S ∧ 1 ∉ S := by
    88→  simp [isPrimitive_iff, hp.properDivisors]
    89→
    90→end Set
    91→

    16→import Mathlib.Tactic
    17→
    18→namespace Nat
    19→
    20→/--
    21→A [perfect power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perfect_power) is a natural number that
    22→  is a product of equal natural factors, or, in other words, an integer that can be expressed
    23→  as a square or a higher integer power of another integer greater than one. More formally,
    24→  $n$ is a perfect power if there exist natural numbers $m > 1$, and $k > 1$ such that
    25→  $m ^ k = n$. In this case, $n$ may be called a perfect $k$th power. If $k = 2$ or
    26→  $k = 3$, then $n$ is called a perfect square or perfect cube, respectively.
    27→-/
    28→def IsPerfectPower (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    29→  ∃ k m : ℕ, 1 < k ∧ 1 < m ∧ k ^ m = n
    30→
    31→theorem isPerfectPower_iff_factorization_gcd (n : ℕ) :
    32→    IsPerfectPower n ↔ n > 1 ∧ n.primeFactors.gcd n.factorization > 1 := by
    33→  constructor
    34→  · -- Forward direction: IsPerfectPower n → n > 1 ∧ gcd > 1
    35→    intro ⟨k, m, hk, hm, hpow⟩
    36→    constructor
    37→    · -- n > 1
    38→      have h : n ≥ 4 := by
    39→        calc n = k ^ m := hpow.symm
    40→          _ ≥ 2 ^ m := Nat.pow_le_pow_left hk m
    41→          _ ≥ 2 ^ 2 := Nat.pow_le_pow_right (by norm_num : 1 ≤ 2) hm
    42→      omega
    43→    · -- gcd > 1
    44→      subst hpow
    45→      have hk_pos : k ≠ 0 := Nat.one_le_iff_ne_zero.mp (le_of_lt hk)
    46→      have hm_pos : m ≠ 0 := Nat.one_le_iff_ne_zero.mp (le_of_lt hm)
    47→      rw [Nat.factorization_pow, primeFactors_pow k hm_pos]
    48→      -- m divides every exponent, so gcd ≥ m > 1
    49→      have h_nonempty := nonempty_primeFactors.mpr hk
    50→      obtain ⟨p, hp⟩ := h_nonempty
    51→      -- Show m divides the gcd
    52→      have h_m_dvd_gcd : m ∣ k.primeFactors.gcd (m • k.factorization) := by
    53→        apply Finset.dvd_gcd
    54→        intro b _
    55→        show m ∣ (m • k.factorization) b
    56→        simp only [Finsupp.coe_smul, Pi.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul]
    57→        exact dvd_mul_right m _
    58→      -- The gcd is positive because k has at least one prime factor with positive exponent
    59→      have hp_in_support : p ∈ k.factorization.support := by
    60→        rw [support_factorization]; exact hp
    61→      have hp_exp_pos : 0 < k.factorization p := Finsupp.mem_support_iff.mp hp_in_support |> Nat.pos_of_ne_zero
    62→      have h_gcd_pos : 0 < k.primeFactors.gcd (m • k.factorization) := by
    63→        have h1 := Finset.gcd_dvd (f := (m • k.factorization)) hp
    64→        have h_eq : (m • k.factorization) p = m * k.factorization p := by
    65→          simp only [Finsupp.coe_smul, Pi.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul]
    66→        rw [h_eq] at h1
    67→        have h_val_pos : 0 < m * k.factorization p := Nat.mul_pos (Nat.pos_of_ne_zero hm_pos) hp_exp_pos
    68→        exact Nat.pos_of_dvd_of_pos h1 h_val_pos
    69→      exact Nat.lt_of_lt_of_le hm (Nat.le_of_dvd h_gcd_pos h_m_dvd_gcd)
    70→  · -- Backward direction: n > 1 ∧ gcd > 1 → IsPerfectPower n
    71→    intro ⟨hn, hgcd⟩
    72→    set g := n.primeFactors.gcd n.factorization with hg_def
    73→    -- Define k as the product of p^(n.factorization p / g) for all primes p | n
    74→    let f : ℕ → ℕ := fun q => q ^ (n.factorization q / g)
    75→    set k := n.primeFactors.prod f with hk_def
    76→    use k, g
    77→    refine ⟨?_, hgcd, ?_⟩
    78→    · -- k > 1
    79→      have h_nonempty := nonempty_primeFactors.mpr hn
    80→      obtain ⟨p, hp⟩ := h_nonempty
    81→      have hp_prime := prime_of_mem_primeFactors hp
    82→      -- p ∈ n.primeFactors means n.factorization p > 0
    83→      have hp_in_support : p ∈ n.factorization.support := by
    84→        rw [support_factorization]; exact hp
    85→      have hp_pos : 0 < n.factorization p := Finsupp.mem_support_iff.mp hp_in_support |> Nat.pos_of_ne_zero
    86→      have h_div : g ∣ n.factorization p := Finset.gcd_dvd hp
    87→      have hg_pos : 0 < g := Nat.lt_trans Nat.zero_lt_one hgcd
    88→      have h_quot_pos : 0 < n.factorization p / g :=
    89→        Nat.div_pos (Nat.le_of_dvd hp_pos h_div) hg_pos
    90→      have h_ge_p : k ≥ f p := by
    91→        rw [hk_def]
    92→        exact Finset.single_le_prod' (f := f)
    93→          (fun q hq => Nat.one_le_pow _ _ (Prime.one_le (prime_of_mem_primeFactors hq))) hp
    94→      have h_ge_2 : f p ≥ 2 := by
    95→        show p ^ (n.factorization p / g) ≥ 2
    96→        calc p ^ (n.factorization p / g) ≥ p ^ 1 := Nat.pow_le_pow_right (Prime.one_le hp_prime) h_quot_pos
    97→          _ = p := pow_one p
    98→          _ ≥ 2 := Prime.two_le hp_prime
    99→      omega
   100→    · -- k ^ g = n

    17→import Mathlib.Algebra.Squarefree.Basic

    19→variable {M M₀ : Type*} {r m : M} {k : ℕ}
    20→
    21→section Monoid
    22→variable [Monoid M]
    23→
    24→def Powerfree (k : ℕ) (m : M) : Prop :=
    25→  ∀ ⦃x : M⦄, x ^ k ∣ m → IsUnit x
    26→
    27→theorem Powerfree.of_le {a b : ℕ} (hab : a ≤ b) (hm : Powerfree a m) :
    28→  Powerfree b m := fun x hx => hm (dvd_of_mul_right_dvd (pow_mul_pow_sub x hab ▸ hx))
    29→
    30→theorem Powerfree.of_dvd (hrm : r ∣ m) (hm : Powerfree k m) :
    31→    Powerfree k r := fun _ hx => hm (hx.trans hrm)
    32→
    33→theorem powerfree_congr (hrm : Associated r m) : Powerfree k r ↔ Powerfree k m :=
    34→  ⟨fun h => h.of_dvd hrm.dvd', fun h => h.of_dvd hrm.dvd⟩
    35→
    36→@[simp]
    37→theorem powerfree_neg [HasDistribNeg M] : Powerfree k (-m) ↔ Powerfree k m :=
    38→  powerfree_congr (Associated.refl m).neg_left
    39→
    40→@[simp]
    41→theorem powerfree_two {m : M} : Powerfree 2 m ↔ Squarefree m where
    42→  mp h x hx := h (sq x ▸ hx)
    43→  mpr h x hx := h x (sq x ▸ hx)
    44→
    45→end Monoid
    46→
    47→section CommMonoid
    48→variable [CommMonoid M]
    49→
    50→theorem IsUnit.powerfree (h : IsUnit m) (hk : k ≠ 0) :
    51→    Powerfree k m := fun _ hx => (isUnit_pow_iff hk).1 (isUnit_of_dvd_unit hx h)
    52→
    53→@[simp]
    54→theorem powerfree_one (hk : k ≠ 0) : Powerfree k (1 : M) :=
    55→  isUnit_one.powerfree hk
    56→
    57→theorem Irreducible.powerfree (h : Irreducible m) (hk : 2 ≤ k) :
    58→    Powerfree k m := by
    59→  rintro y ⟨z, hz⟩
    60→  induction k with
    61→  | zero => grind
    62→  | succ n ih =>
    63→  rw [← npow_eq_pow, Monoid.npow_succ, mul_assoc] at hz
    64→  rcases h.isUnit_or_isUnit hz with (hu | hu)
    65→  · exact (isUnit_pow_iff (by linarith)).1 hu
    66→  · apply isUnit_of_mul_isUnit_left hu
    67→
    68→end CommMonoid
    69→
    70→@[simp]
    71→theorem not_powerfree_zero [MonoidWithZero M₀] [Nontrivial M₀] (k : ℕ) :
    72→    ¬ Powerfree k (0 : M₀) := by

    74→  exact ⟨0, by simp⟩
    75→
    76→theorem Prime.powerfree [CommMonoidWithZero M₀] [IsCancelMulZero M₀] {m : M₀} (h : Prime m)
    77→    (hk : 2 ≤ k) : Powerfree k m := h.irreducible.powerfree hk

    16→import Mathlib.Data.Finset.Basic
    17→import Mathlib.Data.Fintype.Card
    18→import Mathlib.Data.Nat.Lattice
    19→
    20→/-!
    21→# Hypergraph Ramsey Numbers
    22→
    23→This file defines the `r`-uniform hypergraph Ramsey number `R_r(n)`.
    24→-/
    25→
    26→namespace Combinatorics
    27→
    28→/--
    29→The `r`-uniform hypergraph Ramsey number `R_r(n)`.
    30→The smallest natural number `m` such that every 2-coloring of the `r`-subsets of a set of size `m`
    31→contains a monochromatic subset of size `n`.
    32→-/
    33→noncomputable def hypergraphRamsey (r n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    34→  sInf { m | ∀ (c : Finset (Fin m) → Bool),
    35→    ∃ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = n ∧
    36→      ∃ (color : Bool), ∀ (e : Finset (Fin m)), e ⊆ S → e.card = r → c e = color }
    37→
    38→/-- `n ≤ hypergraphRamsey r n` when the set is nonempty. -/
    39→theorem le_hypergraphRamsey (r n : ℕ) (hne : { m | ∀ (c : Finset (Fin m) → Bool),
    40→    ∃ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = n ∧
    41→      ∃ (color : Bool), ∀ (e : Finset (Fin m)), e ⊆ S → e.card = r → c e = color }.Nonempty) :
    42→    n ≤ hypergraphRamsey r n := by
    43→  apply le_csInf hne
    44→  intro m hm
    45→  have : ∃ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = n ∧ _ := hm (fun _ ↦ false)
    46→  obtain ⟨S, hS, -⟩ := this
    47→  calc n = S.card := hS.symm
    48→    _ ≤ Fintype.card (Fin m) := Finset.card_le_univ S
    49→    _ = m := Fintype.card_fin m
    50→
    51→/-- `hypergraphRamsey r r = r` for any `r`. -/
    52→theorem hypergraphRamsey_self (r : ℕ) : hypergraphRamsey r r = r := by
    53→  have h_mem : r ∈ { m | ∀ (c : Finset (Fin m) → Bool),
    54→      ∃ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = m ∧
    55→        ∃ (color : Bool), ∀ (e : Finset (Fin m)), e ⊆ S → e.card = r → c e = color } := by
    56→    intro c
    57→    refine ⟨Finset.univ, ?_, c Finset.univ, ?_⟩
    58→    · simp [Fintype.card_fin]
    59→    · intro e _ he_card
    60→      congr 1
    61→      exact Finset.eq_univ_of_card e (he_card.trans (Fintype.card_fin r).symm)
    62→  exact le_antisymm (Nat.sInf_le h_mem) (le_hypergraphRamsey r r ⟨r, h_mem⟩)
    63→
    64→end Combinatorics
    65→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 264
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/264](https://www.erdosproblems.com/264)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→namespace Erdos264
    26→
    27→open Filter
    28→
    29→open scoped ENNReal Asymptotics
    30→
    31→/--
    32→A sequence $a_n$ of integers is called an irrationality sequence if for every bounded sequence of integers $b_n$ with $a_n + b_n \neq 0$ and
    33→$b_n \neq 0$ for all $n$, the sum
    34→$$
    35→  \sum \frac{1}{a_n + b_n}
    36→$$
    37→is irrational.
    38→
    39→Note: there are other possible definitions of this concept. See
    40→FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/263.lean for another possible definition.
    41→-/
    42→def IsIrrationalitySequence (a : ℕ → ℕ) : Prop := ∀ b : ℕ → ℕ, BddAbove (Set.range b) →
    43→  0 ∉ Set.range (a + b) → 0 ∉ Set.range b → Irrational (∑' n, (1 : ℝ) / (a n + b n))
    44→
    45→/--
    46→Is $2^n$ an example of an irrationality sequence? Kovač and Tao proved that it is not [KoTa24]
    47→
    48→[KoTa24] Kovač, V. and Tao T., On several irrationality problems for Ahmes series. arXiv:2406.17593 (2024).
    49→-/
    50→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    51→theorem erdos_264.parts.i : ¬IsIrrationalitySequence (2 ^ ·) := by sorry
    52→
    53→/--
    54→Is $n!$ an example of an irrationality sequence?
    55→-/
    56→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    57→theorem erdos_264.parts.ii : answer(sorry) ↔ IsIrrationalitySequence Nat.factorial := by sorry
    58→
    59→/--
    60→One example is $2^{2^n}$.
    61→-/
    62→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    63→theorem erdos_264.variants.example : IsIrrationalitySequence (fun n ↦ 2 ^ (2 ^ n)) := by sorry
    64→
    65→/--
    66→Kovač and Tao [KoTa24] generally proved that any strictly increasing sequence of positive integers
    67→$a_n$ such that $\sum \frac{1}{a_n}$ converges and
    68→$$
    69→  \liminf_{n \to \infty} (a_n^2 \sum_{k > n} \frac{1}{a_k^2}) > 0
    70→$$
    71→is not an irrationality sequence.
    72→
    73→[KoTa24] Kovač, V. and Tao T., On several irrationality problems for Ahmes series. arXiv:2406.17593 (2024).
    74→-/
    75→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    76→theorem erdos_264.variants.ko_tao_neg {a : ℕ → ℕ} (h₁ : StrictMono a) (h₂ : 0 ∉ Set.range a)
    77→    (h₃ : Summable ((1 : ℝ) / a ·))
    78→    (h₄ : 0 < atTop.liminf fun n ↦ a n ^ 2 * ∑' k : Set.Ioi n, (1 : ℝ) / a k ^ 2) :
    79→    ¬IsIrrationalitySequence a := by
    80→  sorry
    81→
    82→/--
    83→On the other hand, Kovač and Tao [KoTa24] do prove that for any function $F$ with
    84→$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{F(n + 1)}{F(n)} = \infty$ there exists such an irrationality sequence with $a_n \sim F(n)$.
    85→
    86→[KoTa24] Kovač, V. and Tao T., On several irrationality problems for Ahmes series. arXiv:2406.17593 (2024).
    87→-/
    88→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    89→theorem erdos_264.variants.ko_tao_pos {F : ℕ → ℕ}
    90→    (hF : atTop.Tendsto (fun n ↦ (F (n + 1) : ℝ) / F n) atTop) :
    91→    ∃ a : ℕ → ℕ, IsIrrationalitySequence a ∧ (fun n ↦ (a n : ℝ)) ~[atTop] fun n ↦ (F n : ℝ) := by
    92→  sorry
    93→
    94→end Erdos264
    95→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 293
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/293](https://www.erdosproblems.com/293)
    23→
    24→Let $k \geq 1$ and let $v(k)$ be the minimal integer which does not appear as some $n_i$
    25→in a solution to $1 = 1/n_1 + \cdots + 1/n_k$ with $1 \leq n_1 < \cdots < n_k$.
    26→Estimate the growth of $v(k)$.
    27→
    28→Known bounds:
    29→- $v(k) \gg k!$ (Bleicher–Erdős [BlEr75])
    30→- $v(k) \geq e^{ck^2}$ for some $c > 0$ (van Doorn–Tang [vDTa25b])
    31→- $v(k) \leq k \cdot c_0^{2^k}$ where $c_0 \approx 1.26408$ is the Vardi constant
    32→
    33→It may be that $v(k)$ grows doubly exponentially in $\sqrt{k}$ or even $k$.
    34→
    35→[BlEr75] Bleicher, M.N. and Erdős, P., *Denominators of Egyptian fractions*, J. Number Theory (1975).
    36→
    37→[vDTa25b] van Doorn, F. and Tang, R. (2025).
    38→
    39→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    40→theory*, Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathématique (1980).
    41→-/
    42→
    43→open BigOperators Real Filter Finset Classical
    44→
    45→namespace Erdos293
    46→
    47→/-- A positive integer $n$ appears in a $k$-term Egyptian fraction representation of $1$
    48→if there exists a $k$-element set $S$ of distinct positive integers with $n \in S$
    49→such that $\sum_{m \in S} 1/m = 1$. -/
    50→def AppearsInKTermEgyptian (k n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    51→  ∃ S : Finset ℕ, S.card = k ∧ n ∈ S ∧ (∀ m ∈ S, 0 < m) ∧
    52→    ∑ m ∈ S, (1 : ℚ) / (m : ℚ) = 1
    53→
    54→/-- $v(k)$ is the smallest positive integer that does not appear in any representation
    55→of $1$ as a sum of exactly $k$ distinct unit fractions. -/
    56→noncomputable def v (k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    57→  sInf {n : ℕ | 0 < n ∧ ¬AppearsInKTermEgyptian k n}
    58→
    59→/-- Bleicher–Erdős lower bound [BlEr75]:
    60→There exists a constant $c > 0$ such that $v(k) \geq c \cdot k!$ for all $k \geq 1$. -/
    61→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    62→theorem erdos_293.variants.bleicher_erdos_lower :
    63→    ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ ∀ k : ℕ, 1 ≤ k →
    64→      c * (Nat.factorial k : ℝ) ≤ (v k : ℝ) := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→/-- van Doorn–Tang lower bound [vDTa25b]:
    68→There exists a constant $c > 0$ such that $v(k) \geq e^{ck^2}$ for all
    69→sufficiently large $k$. -/
    70→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    71→theorem erdos_293.variants.van_doorn_tang_lower :
    72→    ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ ∀ᶠ k : ℕ in atTop,
    73→      exp (c * (k : ℝ) ^ 2) ≤ (v k : ℝ) := by
    74→  sorry
    75→
    76→/-- Upper bound: $v(k) \leq k \cdot c_0^{2^k}$ where $c_0$ is the Vardi constant
    77→($\approx 1.26408$).
    78→There exists $c_0 > 1$ such that $v(k) \leq k \cdot c_0^{2^k}$ for all $k \geq 1$. -/
    79→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    80→theorem erdos_293.variants.upper_bound :
    81→    ∃ c₀ : ℝ, 1 < c₀ ∧ ∀ k : ℕ, 1 ≤ k →
    82→      (v k : ℝ) ≤ (k : ℝ) * c₀ ^ ((2 : ℝ) ^ (k : ℕ)) := by
    83→  sorry
    84→
    85→/--
    86→Erdős Problem 293 [ErGr80, p.35]:
    87→
    88→Conjecture: $v(k)$ grows doubly exponentially in $k$. That is, there exists a
    89→constant $c > 0$ such that $v(k) \geq e^{e^{ck}}$ for all sufficiently large $k$.
    90→
    91→(A weaker form conjectures doubly exponential growth in $\sqrt{k}$.)
    92→-/
    93→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    94→theorem erdos_293 :
    95→    ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ ∀ᶠ k : ℕ in atTop,
    96→      exp (exp (c * (k : ℝ))) ≤ (v k : ℝ) := by
    97→  sorry
    98→
    99→end Erdos293
   100→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 318
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→  - [erdosproblems.com/318](https://www.erdosproblems.com/318)
    24→  - [ErSt75] Erdős, P. and Straus, E. G., Solution to Problem 387. Nieuw Arch. Wisk. (1975), 183.
    25→  - [Sa75] Sattler, R., Solution to Problem 387. Nieuw Arch. Wisk. (1975), 184-189.
    26→  - [Sa82b] Sattler, R., On Erdős property P₁ for the arithmetical sequence. Nederl. Akad. Wetensch.
    27→    Indag. Math. (1982), 347--352.
    28→  - [ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    29→    theory. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Set Real
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos318
    35→
    36→/-- A set `A : Set ℕ` is said to have propery `P₁` if for any nonconstant sequence
    37→`f : A → {-1, 1}`, one can always select a finite, nonempty subset `S ⊆ A \ {0}` such that
    38→`∑ n ∈ S, fₙ / n = 0`. This is defined in [Sa82b]. -/
    39→def P₁ (A : Set ℕ) : Prop := ∀ (f : ℕ → ℝ),
    40→  f ∘ (Subtype.val : (A \ {0} : Set ℕ) → ℕ) ≠ (fun _ => 1) →
    41→  f ∘ (Subtype.val : (A \ {0} : Set ℕ) → ℕ) ≠ (fun _ => - 1) →
    42→  Set.range f ⊆ {1, -1} →
    43→  ∃ S : Finset ℕ, S.Nonempty ∧ ↑S ⊆ A \ {0} ∧ ∑ n ∈ S, f n / n = 0
    44→
    45→/-- `ℕ` has property `P₁`. This is proved in [ErSt75]. -/
    46→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    47→theorem erdos_318.univ : P₁ univ := by
    48→  sorry
    49→
    50→/-- Sattler proved in [Sa75] that the set of odd numbers has property `P₁`. -/
    51→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    52→theorem erdos_318.odd : P₁ {n | Odd n} := by
    53→  sorry
    54→
    55→/-- The set of squares does not have property `P₁`. -/
    56→@[category test, AMS 11]
    57→theorem erdos_318.squares : ¬ P₁ ({n | IsSquare n}) := by
    58→  simp only [P₁, not_forall, not_exists, not_and]
    59→  -- Consider the function `f` that sends `1` to `1` and sends all other numbers to `-1`.
    60→  refine ⟨fun n => if n = 1 then 1 else - 1, fun h => ?_, fun h => ?_,
    61→    fun x ⟨y, hy⟩ => ?_, fun S h hs => ?_⟩
    62→  · have : (- 1 : ℝ) = 1 := by simpa using congr_fun h ⟨4, ⟨⟨2, by grind⟩, by grind⟩⟩
    63→    grind
    64→  · have : 1 = (- 1 : ℝ) := by simpa using congr_fun h ⟨1, ⟨IsSquare.one, by grind⟩⟩
    65→    grind
    66→  · by_cases x = 1 <;> grind
    67→  -- Consider two cases: `1 ∈ S` or `1 ∉ S`. In the first case, the finite sum over `S` is bounded
    68→  -- below by `1 - (π ^ 2 / 6 - 1)`, which is positive. In the second case, the finite sum over `S`
    69→  -- is negative.
    70→  by_cases h1 : 1 ∈ S
    71→  · rw [Finset.sum_eq_add_sum_diff_singleton h1, Finset.sum_congr rfl
    72→      (g := fun n : ℕ => (- 1 : ℝ) / n)]
    73→    · simp only [↓reduceIte, Nat.cast_one, div_self one_ne_zero, ← ne_eq, div_eq_mul_one_div
    74→        (- 1 : ℝ), ← Finset.mul_sum, neg_one_mul (∑ x ∈ S \ {1}, 1 / (x : ℝ)), ← sub_eq_add_neg]
    75→      apply ne_of_gt
    76→      calc
    77→      _ < 1 - (π ^ 2 / 6 - 1) := by
    78→        have : π ^ 2 < 3.15 ^ 2 := by gcongr; exact Real.pi_lt_d2
    79→        linarith
    80→      _ = 1 - (∑' n : ℕ, 1 / (n : ℝ) ^ 2 - 1) := by congr; exact hasSum_zeta_two.tsum_eq.symm
    81→      _ ≤ 1 - ∑ n ∈ S \ {1}, 1 / (n : ℝ) := by
    82→        gcongr
    83→        have : 1 = 1 / ((1 : ℕ) : ℝ) := by norm_cast; grind
    84→        nth_rewrite 3 [this]
    85→        rw [le_sub_iff_add_le, ← Finset.sum_eq_sum_diff_singleton_add h1]
    86→        let S' := S.preimage (· ^ 2) (Function.Injective.injOn
    87→          (Nat.pow_left_injective (by decide)))
    88→        have hS' : S'.map ⟨(· ^ 2), Nat.pow_left_injective (by decide)⟩ = S := by
    89→          apply Finset.coe_injective
    90→          have h : (S : Set ℕ) ⊆ Set.range (· ^ 2) :=
    91→            hs.trans (by simp [isSquare_iff_exists_sq, Set.subset_def])
    92→          simpa [S', Set.image_preimage_eq_iff] using h
    93→        rw [← hS', Finset.sum_map, Function.Embedding.coeFn_mk]
    94→        simpa [Nat.cast_pow] using Summable.sum_le_tsum S' (fun _ _ => by positivity) (by simp)
    95→    · intro _ _; grind
    96→  · suffices ∑ n ∈ S, (fun n ↦ if n = 1 then 1 else - 1) n / (n : ℝ) < 0 from by linarith
    97→    refine Finset.sum_neg (fun p hp => ?_) h
    98→    have : p ≠ 1 := by grind
    99→    simp_all [neg_div, zero_lt_iff, (not_iff_not.2 mem_singleton_iff).1 (hs hp).2]
   100→
   101→/-- For any set `A` containing exactly one even number, `A` does not have property `P₁`. Sattler
   102→[Sa82] credits this observation to Erdős, who presumably found this after [ErGr80]. -/
   103→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   104→theorem erdos_318.contain_single_even {A : Set ℕ} (hA : {n | n ∈ A ∧ Even n}.ncard = 1) :
   105→    ¬ P₁ {n | IsSquare n} := by
   106→  sorry
   107→

   109→#TODO: prove this lemma by assuming `erdos_318.contain_single_even`. -/
   110→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   111→theorem erdos_318.posDensity : ∃ A : Set ℕ, HasPosDensity A ∧ ¬ P₁ A := by
   112→  sorry
   113→
   114→/-- Every infinite arithmetic progression has property `P₁`. This is proved in [Sa82b]. -/
   115→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   116→theorem erdos_318.infinite_AP {A : Set ℕ} (hA : A.IsAPOfLength ⊤) : P₁ A := by
   117→  sorry
   118→
   119→/-- Does the set of squares excluding 1 have property `P₁`? -/
   120→@[category research open, AMS 11]
   121→theorem erdos_318.square_excluding_one : answer(sorry) ↔  P₁ ({n | IsSquare n} \ {1}) := by
   122→  sorry
   123→
   124→end Erdos318

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 374
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/374](https://www.erdosproblems.com/374)
    23→
    24→For a natural number $m$, let $F(m)$ be the minimal $k \ge 2$ such that there exist
    25→$a_1 < \cdots < a_k = m$ with $a_1! \cdots a_k!$ a perfect square, and let $D_k = \{m : F(m) = k\}$.
    26→The conjecture asserts that $D_6$ has positive lower density, i.e., $|D_6 \cap \{1,\ldots,n\}| \gg n$.
    27→
    28→[ErGr76] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., _On products of factorials_. Bull. Inst. Math. Acad. Sinica
    29→(1976).
    30→
    31→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    32→theory_. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open scoped BigOperators
    36→open Filter Classical
    37→
    38→namespace Erdos374
    39→
    40→/--
    41→There exists a set of $k$ distinct natural numbers with maximum element $m$
    42→whose product of factorials is a perfect square.
    43→Formally: there exist $a_1 < \cdots < a_k = m$ with $a_1! \cdots a_k!$ a square.
    44→-/
    45→def HasFactorialSquareSeq (m : ℕ) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
    46→  ∃ (s : Finset ℕ), s.card = k ∧ k ≥ 2 ∧ m ∈ s ∧
    47→    (∀ x ∈ s, x ≤ m) ∧
    48→    IsSquare (∏ x ∈ s, Nat.factorial x)
    49→
    50→/--
    51→$m \in D_k$: the minimal factorial-square sequence length for $m$ is exactly $k$.
    52→$F(m) = k$ where $F(m)$ is the minimal $k \ge 2$ such that there exist
    53→$a_1 < \cdots < a_k = m$ with $a_1! \cdots a_k!$ a perfect square.
    54→-/
    55→def IsInD (k : ℕ) (m : ℕ) : Prop :=
    56→  HasFactorialSquareSeq m k ∧ ∀ j, j < k → ¬HasFactorialSquareSeq m j
    57→
    58→/--
    59→Erdős Problem 374 [ErGr76][ErGr80]:
    60→
    61→For $m \in \mathbb{N}$, let $F(m)$ be the minimal $k \ge 2$ such that there exist
    62→$a_1 < \cdots < a_k = m$ with $a_1! \cdots a_k!$ a perfect square.
    63→Let $D_k = \{m : F(m) = k\}$.
    64→
    65→Conjecture: $|D_6 \cap \{1, \ldots, n\}| \gg n$.
    66→
    67→That is, there exists $c > 0$ such that for all sufficiently large $n$,
    68→the count of $m \le n$ with $F(m) = 6$ is at least $c \cdot n$.
    69→-/
    70→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    71→theorem erdos_374 :
    72→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    73→      ∀ᶠ n : ℕ in atTop,
    74→        (((Finset.Icc 1 n).filter (fun m => IsInD 6 m)).card : ℝ) ≥ c * (n : ℝ) := by
    75→  sorry
    76→
    77→end Erdos374
    78→

1→/-

    16→
    17→import Mathlib.Data.Nat.Squarefree
    18→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Data.Nat.Factorization.Basic
    19→
    20→namespace Nat
    21→
    22→/-- `n.squarefreePart` is the unique `a₀ : ℕ` such that `a₀` is squarefree and `n = a₀ * b ^ 2`,
    23→for some `b : ℕ`. At `0` this property is not well defined because `b` must be `0` and `a₀` can be
    24→any squarefree number; we give the junk value `1` at `n = 0` following the convention that the
    25→squarefree part of any square is `1`. -/
    26→def squarefreePart (n : ℕ) : ℕ := n.factorization.prod fun (p e : ℕ) ↦ p ^ (e % 2)
    27→
    28→example : squarefreePart 2 = 2 := by
    29→  decide +native
    30→
    31→example : squarefreePart 5 = 5 := by
    32→  decide +native
    33→
    34→example : squarefreePart 4 = 1 := by
    35→  decide +native
    36→
    37→example : squarefreePart 8 = 2 := by
    38→  decide +native
    39→
    40→example : squarefreePart 16 = 1 := by
    41→  decide +native
    42→
    43→example : squarefreePart 24 = 6 := by
    44→  decide +native
    45→
    46→theorem squarefreePart_ne_zero (n : ℕ) : n.squarefreePart ≠ 0 := by
    47→  simp [squarefreePart]
    48→
    49→theorem squarefreePart_zero : squarefreePart 0 = 1 := by
    50→  simp [squarefreePart]
    51→
    52→/-- If `n` is squarefree, then its squarefree part is itself. -/
    53→theorem squarefreePart_of_squarefree {n : ℕ} (hn : Squarefree n) :
    54→    squarefreePart n = n := by
    55→  nth_rw 2 [← n.factorization_prod_pow_eq_self fun _ ↦ by simp_all]
    56→  simp only [squarefreePart, Finsupp.prod, support_factorization]
    57→  exact Finset.prod_congr rfl fun p hp ↦ by
    58→    rw [factorization_eq_one_of_squarefree hn (mem_primeFactors.1 hp).1 (mem_primeFactors.1 hp).2.1]
    59→
    60→/-- The squarefree part of any square is `1`. -/
    61→theorem squarefreePart_of_isSquare {n : ℕ} (hn : IsSquare n) :
    62→    squarefreePart n = 1 := by
    63→  rcases eq_or_ne n 0 with (rfl | h₀); exact squarefreePart_zero
    64→  obtain ⟨r, rfl⟩ := hn
    65→  rw [ne_eq, mul_eq_zero, or_self] at h₀
    66→  rw [squarefreePart, Finsupp.prod, support_factorization,
    67→    Finset.prod_congr rfl fun p hp ↦ by rw [r.factorization_mul h₀ h₀]]
    68→  simp [← two_mul]
    69→
    70→/-- If $n = \prod_p p^{e_p}$ is the prime factorization of $n$, then $\prod_p p^{e_p \pmod{2}}$
    71→is the prime factorization of the squarefree part of $n$. -/
    72→theorem squarefreePart_factorization (n : ℕ) {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime) :

    75→    n.prod_primeFactors_factorization_apply hp (f := fun p n ↦ n.factorization p % 2)

    97→theorem squarefreePart_mul_squarePart (n : ℕ) : n.squarefreePart * n.squarePart = n :=
